refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety; therefore, safety-driven unavailability is acceptable and must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure events, maintenance records) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Safety Shutdowns) / Total Time. Compare against the 0.97 threshold. Conduct root-cause analysis on any unplanned downtime events to identify systemic failure modes.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) and failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine that ingests telemetry from all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), calculates real-time availability metrics, detects anomalies, and triggers predictive maintenance alerts.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across individual subsystems (Vehicle self-monitors, Teleoperation self-monitors, Safety Services self-monitors) with periodic aggregation and reporting to a central dashboard.
DP-C: Implement a redundancy and fault-tolerance architecture at the Vehicle and Teleoperation levels to minimize downtime, combined with a lightweight availability tracker that logs events post-hoc.
DP-D: Establish a preventive maintenance schedule based on historical failure data and manufacturer recommendations, with manual tracking and periodic availability audits.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine that ingests telemetry from all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), calculates real-time availability metrics, detects anomalies, and triggers predictive maintenance alerts.

potential_sources: DP-A requires real-time telemetry feeds from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; coupling is explicit and manageable via well-defined Telemetry interface.
DP-A depends on accurate time synchronization across subsystems; this is a known integration point.
DP-A's predictive maintenance logic may couple to maintenance scheduling workflows (external to NGHE solution scope); recommend clear ownership boundary.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A is preferred over DP-B (distributed monitoring) because centralized analytics enables holistic availability reasoning, anomaly detection across subsystem interactions, and consistent KPI calculation. DP-C (redundancy-first) is complementary but does not directly address the availability KPI measurement requirement. DP-D (preventive maintenance only) is insufficient without real-time monitoring and predictive capability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: All subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can emit telemetry data in real-time or near-real-time.
Assumption: The evaluation period and availability target (0.97) are fixed and agreed with the customer before system deployment.
Risk: If telemetry data quality is poor (missing, delayed, or inaccurate), availability calculations will be unreliable.
Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms require historical failure data; if insufficient data exists, false positives/negatives may occur.
Risk: High safety criticality means that aggressive predictive maintenance (e.g., preemptive shutdowns) could reduce availability if not carefully tuned.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the definition of 'availability' with the customer: (1) Is it measured per-vehicle or across the fleet? (2) Are safety-driven shutdowns counted as unavailability or excluded? (3) What is the acceptable latency for telemetry ingestion (real-time vs. batch)? (4) Who owns the predictive maintenance decision logic—NGHE system or external maintenance management system?

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. The availability monitoring and analytics engine resides at this level to provide holistic system-level KPI tracking and cross-subsystem anomaly detection.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry streams from Vehicle (operational status, sensor data, fault codes), Teleoperation (connection status, latency, operator input), and Safety Services (safety state, alert events). Used to calculate availability metrics and detect anomalies.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound tasking commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, including predictive maintenance directives (e.g., 'schedule maintenance window', 'reduce operational intensity') to prevent predicted failures.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety alert interface with Safety Services. Inbound: safety-driven shutdown signals. Outbound: availability-related safety notifications (e.g., 'system approaching maintenance threshold, safety review recommended').

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment. The availability monitoring engine is a system-level concern that must integrate data from all subsystems. Placing it at the NGHE Solution boundary ensures holistic visibility and prevents fragmented KPI tracking. Minimal diffusion risk: the engine is a cohesive analytics module with clear inputs (Telemetry) and outputs (Tasking, SafetyAlert).

Low containment. Vehicle is a subsystem focused on mechanical and electrical operation. Placing system-level availability analytics in Vehicle would couple the subsystem to Teleoperation and Safety Services data, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion risk: availability logic would be scattered across Vehicle and other subsystems.

Low containment. Teleoperation is a subsystem focused on remote operation and communication. System-level availability analytics do not belong here; Teleoperation should only report its own operational status via Telemetry. High diffusion risk: availability logic would be split between Teleoperation and other subsystems.

Low containment. Safety Services is focused on safety state management and alert generation. While Safety Services may contribute to availability decisions (e.g., safety-driven shutdowns), the overall availability KPI calculation and predictive maintenance logic should not reside in Safety Services. High diffusion risk: mixing safety logic with availability analytics could create coupling and complicate safety certification.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The availability monitoring and analytics engine is a system-level integrator that must have visibility across all subsystems. It is best contained within the NGHE Solution boundary as a dedicated analytics module (e.g., 'Availability Manager' or 'System Health Monitor') that consumes Telemetry, produces Tasking directives, and coordinates with Safety Services via SafetyAlert.
containment_risks: Risk: If the availability engine becomes too complex or tightly coupled to specific subsystem implementations, it may become difficult to evolve or test independently.
Risk: The Telemetry interface must be well-defined and stable; if subsystems change their telemetry schemas, the availability engine must adapt.

Risk: Predictive maintenance logic may require domain expertise (e.g., vehicle failure modes, network latency patterns); if this expertise is not available in the NGHE Solution team, the engine may produce poor predictions.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define a clear 'Availability Manager' module within NGHE Solution with explicit responsibilities: (1) Ingest and validate Telemetry, (2) Calculate availability KPI, (3) Detect anomalies and predict failures, (4) Generate Tasking directives for preventive maintenance, (5) Coordinate with Safety Services for safety-driven shutdowns. Establish a formal interface contract for Telemetry (schema, frequency, latency requirements) to minimize coupling to subsystem implementations.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP provides the mechanisms to measure, track, and improve availability through real-time monitoring, anomaly detection, and predictive maintenance. The coupling check confirms that DP-A is feasible given the available interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). No fundamental mismatch between the functional requirement and the design parameter.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If the availability engine is not clearly scoped as a single module within NGHE Solution, its logic may leak into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services, creating maintenance and testing burden.

Diffusion Risk 2: The Telemetry interface must be standardized across all subsystems; if subsystems emit telemetry in different formats or frequencies, the availability engine will require complex adaptation logic.
Diffusion Risk 3: Predictive maintenance algorithms may require tuning and retraining over time; if ownership of this logic is unclear, it may become orphaned or inconsistently maintained.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution boundary is the correct container for the availability monitoring engine. Containment score of 0.95 reflects high cohesion and minimal diffusion. The engine is a system-level integrator that must not be fragmented across subsystems. Clear module boundaries and interface contracts are essential to maintain this containment.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-004 is feasible via operational telemetry collection and KPI calculation. The NGHE Solution module will emit availability metrics (e.g., daily/weekly/monthly availability percentage) that can be compared against the 0.97 target. Root-cause analysis of downtime events can be traced back to specific subsystems via the Telemetry interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: the NGHE Solution module owns the availability KPI and the analytics engine. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own the generation of accurate, timely telemetry data. The interface contract (Telemetry schema, frequency, latency) must be formally documented to ensure all parties understand their responsibilities.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize the Telemetry interface contract, including: (a) data schema (JSON, protobuf, or other), (b) required fields (timestamp, subsystem ID, status, fault codes), (c) frequency (real-time, 1 Hz, 10 Hz, etc.), (d) latency requirements (e.g., <100 ms), (e) error handling (missing data, out-of-order events).

Revision 2: Define the 'Availability Manager' module within NGHE Solution with explicit responsibilities, interfaces, and ownership. Document the module's internal architecture (e.g., telemetry ingestion, data validation, KPI calculation, anomaly detection, predictive maintenance logic).

Revision 3: Clarify the relationship between availability and safety. Document how safety-driven shutdowns are distinguished from failure-driven unavailability in the KPI calculation. Establish a coordination protocol between the Availability Manager and Safety Services.

Revision 4: Define the evaluation period and customer acceptance criteria in the requirements baseline. Ensure that the 0.97 target is realistic given the system architecture and operational environment.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a high-level KPI requirement that is not atomic. It requires decomposition into lower-level functional requirements (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) that address specific aspects of availability: (1) failure detection and logging, (2) preventive maintenance, (3) status visibility, and (4) safety-driven vs. failure-driven unavailability distinction. The selected DP-A (centralized monitoring and analytics engine) is sound and well-contained within the NGHE Solution module, but the sub-FRs must be refined and decomposed further to guide detailed design and implementation. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to allocate responsibilities to specific modules and define detailed design parameters.
decision.justification_detail: The current level of decomposition has established the system-level strategy (centralized availability monitoring). The next level must address: (1) how to detect and classify failures (FR-NGHE-004.1), (2) how to implement predictive maintenance (FR-NGHE-004.2), (3) how to provide visibility to stakeholders (FR-NGHE-004.3), and (4) how to distinguish safety-driven from failure-driven unavailability (FR-NGHE-004.4). Each of these sub-FRs will have its own DP and IM assignments, potentially involving Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and external systems (e.g., maintenance management system).
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) and failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

assumptions: Assumption 1: All subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can emit telemetry data in real-time or near-real-time via the Telemetry interface.
Assumption 2: The customer-defined availability target (0.97) and evaluation period are fixed and agreed before system deployment.
Assumption 3: Safety-driven shutdowns are acceptable and will be excluded from the unavailability calculation.
Assumption 4: The NGHE Solution team has access to historical failure data and domain expertise to develop predictive maintenance algorithms.
Assumption 5: The Telemetry interface is stable and well-defined; subsystems will not change their telemetry schemas without coordination.
open_risks: Risk 1: Telemetry data quality may be poor (missing, delayed, inaccurate), leading to unreliable availability calculations. Mitigation: Implement data validation and quality checks in the Availability Manager.

Risk 2: Predictive maintenance algorithms may produce false positives/negatives if historical failure data is insufficient. Mitigation: Start with conservative thresholds and tune based on operational experience.

Risk 3: High safety criticality means that aggressive predictive maintenance could reduce availability if not carefully tuned. Mitigation: Establish a safety review process for predictive maintenance directives.

Risk 4: Ownership of predictive maintenance decision logic may be unclear (NGHE system vs. external maintenance management system). Mitigation: Clarify ownership and interface contracts early in design.

Risk 5: The Availability Manager may become a bottleneck or single point of failure if not designed with redundancy and fault tolerance. Mitigation: Consider high-availability architecture for the Availability Manager itself.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Formal Telemetry interface specification (schema, frequency, latency, error handling).
Evidence 2: Historical failure data and root-cause analysis from similar systems or field trials.
Evidence 3: Customer acceptance criteria and evaluation period definition.
Evidence 4: Safety analysis documenting the relationship between availability and safety-driven shutdowns.
Evidence 5: Detailed design of the Availability Manager module, including internal architecture, algorithms, and testing strategy.
Evidence 6: Operational procedures and dashboards for Site Manager and Maintenance team to monitor availability status.
